raw_case:
A ganglioglioma versus a schwannoma: Which is in the sacral spine?


Introduction and importance

Sacral spinal gangliogliomas, rare WHO grade I tumors, present a surgical challenge due to their proximity to critical nerve roots. Symptoms often include lower back pain, sciatica, and bladder dysfunction. Diagnosis is confirmed through MRI and biopsy, with surgical resection being the primary treatment.

Case presentation

A 66-year-old woman presented with left lower limb pain and weakness. MRI showed an intradural lesion at the left S1 root. She underwent surgery, resulting in near-total tumor resection. Postoperatively, she was pain-free and ambulatory. Pathology confirmed a ganglioglioma.

Clinical discussion

Initially suspected as a disc herniation, MRI indicated a schwannoma-like lesion, but surgery and pathology revealed a ganglioglioma. Total resection was challenging due to the tumor's neural adherence. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy are generally not used for low-grade gangliogliomas.

Conclusion

Treating sacral spinal gangliogliomas requires precise surgery and a multidisciplinary approach. Accurate diagnosis and careful planning are essential for minimizing neurological risks and improving patient outcomes. Continued research is crucial for advancing treatment strategies.

Graphical abstract



Highlights

•Rare and challenging tumors: Sacral spinal gangliogliomas are uncommon, low-grade tumors that pose significant surgical challenges due to their location near critical nerve roots.•Common symptoms: Patients often experience lower back pain, sciatica, and bladder dysfunction.•Diagnosis and treatment: MRI and biopsy are used for diagnosis. Surgical resection is the primary treatment, although radiotherapy and chemotherapy may be considered in some cases.•Surgical risks: The surgery can be complex and carries a risk of neurological complications, such as loss of bowel or bladder function.•Long-term outcomes: While the prognosis is generally favorable, long-term outcomes can vary. Recurrence is possible, and some patients may experience persistent symptoms.•Specialized care: Due to their rarity, finding experienced surgeons and healthcare providers who specialize in treating sacral spinal gangliogliomas can be challenging.1

Introduction

The sacral spinal gangliogliomas poses a neuroradiological challenge in surgery. These comparatively uncommon WHO grade I tumors, which form out of neuronal and glial components, are often misunderstood and show symptoms like lower back pain, sciatica, or bladder dysfunctions due to the localization adjacent to critical nerve roots [1,2]. Typically, the diagnosis is done examining a MRI image and ensuring the diagnosis is confirmed by a biopsy. Today the surgical resection is the main treatment application with the purpose of maximal tumor removal with lowest possible risk to neurological function. Nonetheless, total resection could prove to be challenging resulting from the intricacy of sacral spine anatomy not to mention the closeness to essential structures [[3], [4], [5]]. Alongside adjuvant therapies like radiation, surgical or medical treatment can also be explored for any residual or recurrent tumors. The treatment results vary in the long run with resection and tissue sample type, and patient's age. Although gangliogliomas often grow more slowly and their survival prognosis is considered better in comparison with other spinal tumors, it is critical to follow them up regularly due to the risk of recurrence. Interdisciplinary approach including participation of neurosurgeons, oncologists, and rehabilitation specialists is essential for achieving both the best medical results as well as ensuring the best possible life quality of the patients [4]. Concentration of sacral spine ganglioglioma cases in general population is rare, therefore, exact figures about the epidemiology rate are difficult to report. On the other hand, they only constitute a minimal portion of all spinal tumors; the percentage is reported to be less than 1 % of all the cases. Therefore, the proportion of gangliogliomas in sacral spine is less than of the others, even though it is a small fraction of all spinal tumors. This is just the one why personalized approach to therapy and the further studies being done to describe these tumors better will be so important to patients outcomes. In this report, we present the case of a female patient who presented for left sided sciatica and was ultimately diagnosed with a left s1 ganglioglioma.2

Case presentation

A 66-year-old female patient came to the outpatient clinic for left lower limb pain with weakness. The history of her complaint goes back to several years of insidiously increasing lower back pain without radiation to the lower limb. Few weeks prior to presentation, the patient reported new onset left lower limb pain that starts at the anterior thigh and continues into the calf with difficulty to ambulate. No drop foot was noted. The patient reported normal micturition and defecation. The patient scored 51 % on the Oswestry disability index and 6 on the visual analogue scale (VAS) [5,6].A lumbosacral magnetic resonance imaging revealed an intradural foraminal and extraforaminal lesion at the left S1 root. The dimensions of the lesion were 25 mm in the cranial-caudal (CC) axis and 16 mm in the anterio-posterior (AP) axis (Fig. 1, Fig. 2).The patient was scheduled for surgery. Under general anesthesia, in prone position and through a 10 cm linear incision over the midline of the low lumbar spine till the s2 vertebra. Unilateral laminectomy and foraminotomy of the S1 vertebra and root respectively. Under microscopic guidance and with continuous neurostimulation of the sacral plexus nerve roots, durotomy was done and the lesion was dissected in a piecemeal fashion of the S1 nerve root. Near total resection as done. The tumor was whitish-greyish in color and adherent to the nerve root compressing the later against the roof of the foramen. Postoperatively, the patient was pain free, and ambulatory and was discharged 48 h of the procedure. The pathology study revealed that the lesion presented a ganglioglioma (Fig. 3A, Fig. 3B, Fig. 3C, Fig. 3D, Fig. 3E). The sections of the tumor show a proliferation of cells arranged in small short fascicles of varied density and having schwannian appearance. The cells have angular and spindle nucleus and show some nuclear dystrophies, without significant atypia or mitotic activity. These cells are admixed with mature ganglion cells. The immunostaining reveals that the tumor cells are positive for synaptophysin and PS100. There are negative for smooth muscle actin. The proliferation index MIB-1 is moderate. The patient was discharged on a follow up plan.3

Discussion

The presentation of the patient was suggestive of a disc herniation with unilateral nerve compression. MRI revealed a lesion suggestive of a S1 root schwannoma. Differentiation of spinal schwannoma from other lesions such as meningiomas are evident in the literature [1]. MRI feature, clinical assessment and epidemiology aid in the categorization of these tumors (Table 1) [[7], [8], [9]]. However, studies have not established basis for comparison of schwannomas against gangliogliomas. Usually, gangliogliomas of the spine typically involve multiple segments (up to 8 vertebrae) and can show cystic changes in 46 % [2,3]. This patient had distinct MRI features more suggestive of a schwannoma i.e. the lesion was following the left S1 nerve root foraminal trajectory; the lesion was hypointense on T1 and isointense on T2 [10].During surgical excision, the tumor was intradural and intramedullary. The adherence of the tumor to the neural tissue was less suggestive of a benign schwannoma. Gross total resection (GTR) was not feasible due to adherence and invasiveness into the neural tissue even while using spinal neuromonitoring. The literature reports 27 % recurrence rate even with GTR [2]. However, neither radiotherapy (Rx) nor chemotherapy (Cx) have been indicated for the treatment of low grade gangliogliomas. In fact, except for anaplastic grade III gangliogliomas for which Rx is an adjuvant treatment, the latter can cause malignant transformation of low-grade lesions [4,11].4

Conclusion

Sacral spinal gangliogliomas are rare and challenging tumors to treat due to their proximity to critical nerve structures. Successful management requires precise surgical intervention and a multidisciplinary approach to optimize patient outcomes. This case illustrates the importance of accurate diagnosis and careful surgical planning in achieving significant postoperative improvement while minimizing neurological risks. Continued research and case documentation are essential for advancing treatment strategies and improving long-term prognoses for patients with these uncommon tumors.

Disclaimer

The work has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria [12].

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

The ethical committee at Lebanese University exempted this case report from ethical clearance as all data is anonymously reported.

Funding

There are no funding sources.

Author contribution

Ali Msheik: Study concept, design, data collection, writing the paper.Elie Fahed: Study concept, design, data collection, writing the paper.Delivrance Sebaaly: Data collection, Data Analysis, Manuscript Writing, Final Manuscript Editing.Gerard Abadjian: Data collection, Data Analysis, Manuscript Writing, Final Manuscript Editing.Philippe Younes: Study concept, design, data collection, writing the paper, supervision.

Guarantor

Ali Msheik is the guarantor.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 66-year-old female
- Chief Complaint: Left lower limb pain and weakness
- History of Present Illness: Chronic history of progressively worsening lower back pain without radiation; recent onset of left-sided anterior thigh and calf pain, with difficulty ambulating.
- Past Medical History: None reported
- Personal History: None reported
- Family History: None reported
- Physical Examination: Demonstrated left lower limb weakness without foot drop; normal bladder and bowel function.
- Ancillary Tests: MRI revealed an intradural lesion at the left S1 nerve root (25mm craniocaudal and 16mm anteroposterior), with hypointensity on T1 and isointensity on T2. Pathology after surgery revealed the lesion to be a ganglioglioma, confirmed through histopathological findings and immunostaining (positive for synaptophysin and PS100; negative for smooth muscle actin).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- The patient's symptoms and MRI findings initially suggested conditions like disc herniation or sacral schwannoma. Sciatica-type pain and location-specific nerve compression raised suspicion for a schwannoma. MRI revealed the lesion's intradural and intramedullary nature with nerve adherence, making a benign schwannoma in this context less likely.
- Schwannomas generally exhibit different growth patterns and imaging characteristics; the tumor’s neural tissue adherence was atypical for this diagnosis.
- Pathology post-resection ultimately distinguished a ganglioglioma by identifying its combination of neuronal and glial components, spindle nucleo-cytoplasmic structures, and immunohistological profile (synaptophysin, PS100 positivity).
- Differential considerations excluded more invasive tumors due to the absence of significant atypia, mitotic activity, or histology indicative of malignancy.

Pathology after surgery revealed the lesion to be a ganglioglioma, confirmed through histopathological findings and immunostaining (positive for synaptophysin and PS100; negative for smooth muscle actin).

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 66-year-old woman with left lower limb pain and weakness. Her history includes chronic lower back pain that's been getting worse, and now she has pain in the anterior thigh and calf, making it hard to walk. No significant past medical, personal, or family history. On exam, there's left lower limb weakness without foot drop, and normal bladder and bowel function.

First, the MRI shows an intradural lesion at the left S1 nerve root. The size is 25mm craniocaudal and 16mm anteroposterior. The imaging characteristics are hypointensity on T1 and isointensity on T2. Then, after surgery, the pathology came back as a ganglioglioma, confirmed by immunostains positive for synaptophysin and PS100, negative for smooth muscle actin.

So, the first step is to consider the location of the lesion. The S1 nerve root is part of the lumbar spine, so this is a spinal lesion. The symptoms of pain and weakness in the lower limb suggest a possible nerve root compression. But the MRI findings are specific: intradural, which means inside the dura mater, so it's a spinal intradural lesion. The size is notable, but the key is the histopathology.

Ganglioglioma is a type of tumor that's usually found in the brain, but they can occur in the spinal cord as well, though less commonly. The imaging features here—hypointense on T1 and isointense on T2—are somewhat typical for ganglioglioma, though I know that on MRI, gangliogliomas can vary. The presence of both neuronal and glial components (since ganglioglioma is a mix of ganglion cells and glial cells) would explain the immunostains: synaptophysin (neuronal marker) and PS100 (glial marker). The negative smooth muscle actin rules out some other tumors like schwannomas or meningiomas, which might have different markers.

The fact that it's a ganglioglioma is important. These tumors are usually benign but can be slow-growing. The location at S1 might explain the radicular symptoms. The absence of foot drop suggests that the lesion isn't affecting the peroneal nerve, which is more related to L4-L5. However, S1 involvement could affect the sciatic nerve's branches, leading to weakness in the calf and thigh. The normal bladder and bowel function is a good sign, indicating that the conus medullaris or cauda equina isn't involved, which would cause more severe symptoms.

Putting it all together, the MRI findings and the histopathology confirm the diagnosis. Even though ganglioglioma is rare in the spinal cord, the combination of imaging and immunohistochemistry supports this. The patient's symptoms align with a lesion compressing the S1 nerve root, leading to the pain and weakness described.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with progressive left lower limb pain and weakness, localized to the anterior thigh and calf, which correlates with S1 nerve root distribution. The absence of foot drop and normal bladder/bowel function suggests a focal lesion without cauda equina involvement.  
<step 2> MRI reveals an intradural lesion at the left S1 nerve root with specific imaging characteristics (T1 hypointensity, T2 isointensity), which are consistent with a ganglioglioma, a rare neuroglial tumor.  
<step 3> Histopathological confirmation with immunostaining (synaptophysin and PS100 positive, smooth muscle actin negative) definitively identifies the lesion as a ganglioglioma, ruling out other intradural tumors like schwannomas or meningiomas.  
<step 4> The clinical, radiological, and pathological findings align to confirm the diagnosis of spinal ganglioglioma at the S1 nerve root level.  

### Answer:
Spinal ganglioglioma at the left S1 nerve root.
```
